title: Moussaka

overview.description: A comforting Greek bake of layers of aubergine, rich lamb sauce and creamy béchamel, finished with a golden Parmesan topping. Perfect for batch cooking and one of the family's favourite meals.

method: Slice the aubergines into approximately 1cm rounds.
Soak the slices in lightly salted water for 20 minutes, then drain and pat thoroughly dry.
Brush the aubergines lightly with olive oil and roast at 200°C until softened and lightly coloured, turning once.
Meanwhile, heat a little olive oil in a large saucepan and gently cook the onions until soft.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Add the lamb mince and cook until well browned.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes.
Add the chopped tomatoes, cinnamon, oregano and bay leaves.
Simmer gently for around 45 minutes until thick and rich. Remove the bay leaves.
For the béchamel, melt the butter, stir in the flour and cook for 1 minute.
Gradually whisk in the milk until smooth and thickened. Season with salt, pepper and a pinch of nutmeg.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional).
Layer half of the aubergines into a large ovenproof dish.
Spread over all of the meat sauce.
Arrange the remaining aubergines on top.
Pour over the béchamel and spread evenly.
Bake for 40–45 minutes until golden brown and bubbling.
Leave to rest for at least 20 minutes before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Although often thought of as an ancient Greek dish, modern moussaka was popularised in the early 20th century by chef Nikolaos Tselementes, who introduced the French-style béchamel topping.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to prepare aubergines, build a rich meat sauce, make a classic béchamel and assemble layered bakes.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: One of the family's favourite comfort foods and a perfect Saturday project. It freezes beautifully and rewards the extra effort with outstanding flavour.
chapman-tips: Soaking the aubergine briefly in salted water helps reduce oil absorption and improves the finished texture.
Roasting the aubergine gives a lighter result than frying.
Allow the moussaka to rest before serving so the layers hold together neatly.
Freshly grated Parmesan gives the best flavour and colour.
notes.batch-cooking: Bake two dishes while the oven is on and freeze one whole or in individual portions for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Reheat thoroughly in the oven or microwave. Like lasagne, it's often even better the following day.
